original_abstract: As Artificial Intelligence (AI) increasingly permeates higher education across Africa, it brings both transformative potential and significant moral complexities. This study investigates the ethical challenges associated with AI adoption in African universities, focusing on the unique socio-technical landscape of the continent. Utilizing a desk-based methodology, the research synthesizes data from existing academic literature, institutional reports, and online journals to identify recurring ethical vulnerabilities. The findings highlight critical concerns regarding data privacy, the opacity of AI algorithms, and the potential for bias in automated decision-making processes. The study reveals that for African institutions to successfully integrate AI, they must navigate the delicate balance between technological efficiency and the protection of student and faculty rights. Based on these findings, the paper suggests that future research be anchored in critical theory and the "ethics of care" to foster more human-centric AI frameworks. To ensure responsible implementation, the study recommends the integration of ethics education into university curricula and the development of context-specific regulatory policies. Such frameworks should mandate algorithmic transparency and establish robust institutional guidelines for data management, ensuring that AI development aligns with the cultural and ethical values of the African academic community.
humanized_abstract: This study examines the moral dilemmas emanating from the implementation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in higher institutions of learning in Africa, while making reference to the socio-technical context of the continent. While AI in higher institutions of learning in Africa is revolutionising the academic sector, it also raises a number of ethical issues. Drawing on a desk-based research methodology, that synthesised information from journal articles, research articles and institutional reports among others, this study identifies the ethico-moral concerns emanating from the use of AI, most of which have been reported in existing literature. The ethical concerns that were revealed emanated from three broad sources of concern namely data privacy issues, lack of accountability due to the opacity of the AI system as well as issues of discrimination in as far as the students and staff are concerned, emanating from bias in the decisions made by the technology. In order to make the use of AI in higher learning institutions in Africa a success, the stakeholders in the sector have to ensure that they adhere to the tenets of fairness, equity and justice and also ensure that the rights of the students and staff are guaranteed, while ensuring that the technology is also efficient. This study therefore recommends that, any future studies conducted in this area should be anchored on the principles of critical theory and ethics of care that will ensure the humanity of the technology. In addition to that, the study recommends that ethics education concerning the use of AI should be taught in the institutions and that policies should be put in place that ensures that the technological innovation is implemented in a manner that reflects the African values and ethics. The policies will have to entail, among others, issues of accountability as far as the technology is concerned, ensuring transparency of the working of the algorithm, as well as rules and regulations that are aimed at ensuring proper use of data, amongst others.
